reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Hungarian employers' documented interest in reducing credential sorting by family background (to access talent across SES strata) and reducing test-prep arms-race pressure (to preserve cognitive capacity for learning-to-learn and adaptability). These derive from: (1) employer surveys on skill gaps and recruitment barriers (MKIK, GFK employer surveys); (2) tech-sector statements on diversity and access to diverse problem-solving teams (e.g., Hungarian Chamber of Commerce digital economy briefings); (3) labour-market efficiency framing: early selection reduces the pool of high-ability low-SES workers available for upskilling.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech sector: need a broader, less-SES-filtered pipeline of high-ability entrants into selective secondary and tertiary STEM pathways; need schools to preserve cognitive bandwidth for learning-to-learn rather than test-prep.
reactions.public_good_frame: Efficient allocation of talent: removing SES-correlated sorting mechanisms widens the pool of high-potential workers available for employer investment and reduces deadweight loss from mismatch between ability and opportunity.
reactions.argument: S1 directly addresses the sorting mechanism without structural disruption. A coaching-resistant assessment and funded outreach to low-SES/Roma families can measurably increase the pool of high-ability low-SES pupils entering selective tracks, provided the SES proxy is workable and outreach raises application rates. This serves employer interest in two ways: (i) it widens the talent pipeline by removing SES barriers to selection, increasing the probability that high-ability low-SES pupils reach tracks where employer investment in tertiary STEM pathways is cost-effective; (ii) it reduces the test-prep arms race, preserving classroom time for deeper learning and adaptability — traits employers report as more scarce than test-taking technique. However, the scenario itself flags that the SES proxy is contested (Hungary lacks an administrative student-level SES register usable at application), and the evidence that a redesigned assessment resists coaching is weak (the cited value-added studies do not establish this). Employers benefit only if these mechanisms work; if they fail, the quota system absorbs seats without raising the low-SES pool, and the arms race intensifies on the narrowed residual selective path.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes full ('support' instead of 'conditional') if: (i) pilot evidence demonstrates that the proposed SES proxy has <15% gaming rate and strong predictive validity; (ii) randomized coaching studies show the new assessment format resists preparation by >30% relative to the current exam. Support reverses to 'oppose' if: (i) pilot data show the SES proxy is gameable above 30% or has weak prediction of family-background disadvantage; (ii) outreach pilots fail to raise low-SES application rates by >20%; (iii) residual selective places become even more SES-skewed as the quota fills, suggesting the mechanism is mechanical without addressing peer-composition sorting.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Same as S1: employer interest in broadening the talent pipeline and reducing early sorting. S2's appeal rests on the Hanushek & Woessmann (2006) evidence that earlier tracking increases SES-outcome gradients — a finding employers interpret as evidence that later sorting preserves higher-ability low-SES workers in mainstream tracks longer, extending the window for employer identification and investment. However, the scenario itself flags that caps may be offset by non-state maintainers, and that residual selective places become even more SES-skewed as they scarcen absent an S1-type rule change.
reactions.interest: Same as S1: employers and tech sector need a broader talent pipeline and reduced early sorting.
reactions.public_good_frame: Same as S1: efficient talent allocation; delayed sorting reduces mismatch.
reactions.argument: S2 appeals to employer interest via the delayed-sorting mechanism: keeping more high-achievers in the common track until age 14 theoretically extends the window during which high-ability low-SES pupils remain identifiable and reachable by employer investment. This serves the talent-pipeline framing. However, S2 introduces a critical tension: the scenario acknowledges that caps on state-maintained schools are 'directly contradicted by this scenario's own political risks and uncertainties' — i.e., non-state maintainers will absorb displaced selective demand, relocating rather than reducing sorting. If that offset occurs, the pipeline-widening effect vanishes: the same high-SES pupils still sort into selective tracks via church/foundation channels, and the common track in state schools may become lower-SES-skewed without becoming more diverse by ability. Additionally, the scenario flags that 'as caps tighten, residual selective places become even more SES-skewed absent an S1-type admission-rule change' — a mechanical effect that narrows the employers' accessible pool precisely where seats scarcen. Employers gain only if (a) non-state caps are legally enforced, not negotiated, and (b) residual state-school selective places are reformed via S1 mechanisms. Without both, S2 risks entrenchment: fewer, more SES-skewed selective seats in state schools, while the actual sorting migrates to unregulated non-state channels, reducing employer visibility into the selective pipeline.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes full if: (i) evidence that non-state maintainers will accept intake caps as binding constraints (e.g., regulatory data showing cap compliance in other EU systems; maintainer interviews committing to alignment); (ii) S1 mechanisms are bundled into S2 to reform residual state-school selective admission rules; (iii) pilot data show that high-achiever retention in state-school common tracks actually materializes (longitudinal peer-effect evidence, not model knowledge). Support reverses to 'oppose' if: (i) non-state maintainers expand intake to absorb the capped demand, defeating the pool-widening effect; (ii) residual state-school selective places become demonstrably more SES-skewed during the transition, narrowing the talent pool employers can identify; (iii) legislative enforcement of non-state caps proves infeasible (precedent: Nkt. amendments repeatedly exempt church schools from new rules).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in accessing high-ability talent across SES strata conflicts with the structural risk in S3: middle-class and non-state exit. The Finnish and Polish evidence on delayed tracking supports the equity goal, but the Hungarian implementation risks are severe: (i) teacher shortage (strong, from OECD Education at a Glance and PSZ/PDSZ surveys) undermines the bundled teacher-quality assumption; (ii) non-state maintainers are fragmented and less regulated than Nordic/Baltic providers, so exit risk is higher; (iii) political durability across two electoral cycles contradicts the political_feasibility finding that 'no durable window exists'. Employers depend on a visible, predictable pathway to identify talent; S3's relocation-of-sorting risk obscures that pathway.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech sector: need a stable, visible pipeline of high-ability entrants; need teacher quality sufficient to develop high-potential pupils in mixed-ability classes.
reactions.public_good_frame: Economic stability and talent efficiency: structural reform that fails to prevent exit of high-achievers (and their peer effects) to non-state channels relocates sorting rather than removing it, leaving employers unable to identify broad-based talent and increasing cost to employers via higher private-school concentration.
reactions.argument: S3 represents the highest-risk option from the employer perspective, despite its largest expected aggregate equity gain. The scenario acknowledges — but does not resolve — the largest tension: 'middle-class and church/foundation exit to non-state or private schools…it can relocate rather than remove sorting, so the net equity gain is bounded by how much displacement occurs — a magnitude the inputs do not resolve.' For employers, this relocation is catastrophic: if middle-class pupils and high-achievers migrate to unregulated non-state/private systems, the state comprehensive track becomes lower-SES and lower-ability-skewed, reducing the pool of high-potential pupils employers can recruit from. Additionally, S3 assumes teacher-quality investment sufficient to prevent middle-class flight, yet the scenario itself contradicts this: 'teacher supply can be stabilized enough to staff heterogeneous comprehensive classes [assumption — contradicted by implementation_planning's strong teacher-shortage finding]'. Hungary's documented teacher shortage (OECD Education at a Glance; union surveys) means comprehensive classes will be taught by lower-capacity teachers than selective tracks were, inverting the Finnish bundling logic. Employers depend on predictable, visible pathways to talent; S3's relocation risk and teacher-capacity gap render those pathways invisible and unstable. The political durability risk (reversal after two cycles) compounds this: employers cannot invest in recruitment pipelines that may be restructured within a decade.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes 'conditional' if: (i) regulatory data demonstrate that non-state maintainers in Hungary will not absorb > 15% of displaced selective demand (e.g., binding legislative caps on non-state intake; maintainer registries showing historical intake stability); (ii) teacher-supply forecasts show recruitment and retention sufficient to staff comprehensive classes at quality equivalent to current selective tracks (e.g., salary/working-condition commitments; union agreement on differentiation protocols); (iii) coalition-stability forecasts demonstrate >75% probability of durability across two electoral cycles. Opposition becomes 'support' if all three conditions are met and (iv) pilot evaluation shows within-system sorting by peer effects and school choice actually decreases despite the structural change (i.e., comprehensive tracks retain high-achievers and mixed ability).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in broadening the talent pipeline conflicts directly with S4's core mechanism: retaining age-10/12 sorting while compensating downstream. The Portuguese evidence (Crato 2020; OECD PISA) shows modest attainment gains, but does not demonstrate that downstream compensation can offset early selection at Hungary's selection age. Employers need to access high-ability low-SES workers before they are filtered out at ages 10-12; compensation cannot recover pupils who have already been sorted into lower-SES, lower-expectation tracks.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech sector: need early, structural access to high-ability low-SES pupils before age-10/12 sorting filters them into lower-opportunity tracks.
reactions.public_good_frame: Talent efficiency and economic justice: early sorting is a permanent, not temporary, filter; downstream compensation cannot recover workers who were sorted into tracks with lower peer quality, lower teacher expectations, and lower STEM exposure. Employers depend on structural access, not compensatory repair.
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally misaligned with employer interest because it leaves the sorting mechanism intact at the point where it is most consequential: ages 10-12. The scenario itself flags this: 'Narrows the SES gradient at best partially, since it leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact and only compensates downstream…the ceiling on equity gain is set by the untested offsetting assumption.' For employers, this means the talent pipeline remains SES-filtered at its source. A high-ability low-SES pupil sorted into a general track at age 10 may receive better downstream support, but she will have spent ages 10-14 in a lower-peer-quality, lower-teacher-expectation environment where STEM exposure is typically lower (because selective tracks concentrate STEM resources). By age 14, when selection into tertiary pathways begins, the cumulative disadvantage from early sorting is largely irreversible via compensation alone. The Portuguese case (which S4 models) did not select as early as Hungary (age 10-12); it did not demonstrate offsetting at that age threshold. Employers lose the window to identify and invest in high-potential low-SES pupils before they have been SES-sorted into lower-opportunity tracks. Additionally, S4 explicitly legitimizes early selection: 'Legitimising early selection permanently: compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine [assumption — plausible entrenchment risk].' If early sorting is institutionalized as 'normal' with compensation as the remedy, political will to reform the mechanism disappears, and the pipeline remains narrowed indefinitely. Budget vulnerability compounds this: targeted funding is 'the first line cut in fiscal consolidation,' so the compensatory mechanism is structurally fragile.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes 'conditional' if: (i) Hungarian matched-cohort value-added studies demonstrate that compensatory support actually closes >50% of the SES-outcome gap created by age-10/12 sorting (no such evidence exists; the condition is designed to be unlikely); (ii) legislative ring-fencing of targeted funding survives three fiscal-consolidation cycles without >20% reduction (unlikely given Hungarian budget history); (iii) pilot evidence shows that downstream support measurably increases low-SES pupils' transition rates into selective upper-secondary or STEM tertiary pathways despite their early sorting into general tracks (contradicts the logic of early selection). Opposition becomes 'support' only if all three conditions are met and (iv) evidence emerges that S1 or S2 admission-rule reforms are politically infeasible, making S4 the only durable option available — but even then, support would be 'conditional,' not full, because the mechanism does not structurally address employer interest in early pipeline access.
